readingQuestions.prompt: 本文を読んで各設問に答えてください。

payload.title: The Quiet Return of Night Trains
paragraphs: For decades, overnight trains seemed destined to disappear. Cheap flights made it faster to cross a continent in a few hours, and many night services were quietly cancelled. By the early 2010s, only a handful of routes survived, kept alive more by nostalgia than by demand.

Recently, however, sleeper trains have begun a surprising comeback. As travelers grow more aware of the environmental cost of flying, a growing number choose to spend the night gliding between cities instead. New routes now connect distant capitals, and tickets often sell out weeks in advance. Governments, eager to cut emissions, have started funding services that private companies once abandoned.

Challenges remain. Running comfortable sleeper cars is expensive, and aging tracks can slow journeys to a crawl. Yet supporters argue that the appeal is not only about speed. A night train lets passengers fall asleep in one country and wake in another, saving the cost of a hotel and arriving rested. For many, that quiet, unhurried experience is exactly the point.

questions.prompt: 本文の主旨として最も適切なものはどれですか。
options: Night trains are returning, driven partly by environmental concerns despite real obstacles.
Cheap flights have permanently replaced overnight trains across the continent.
Governments refuse to fund any form of long-distance rail travel.
Sleeper trains are now faster than airplanes on every route.

questions.before: According to the passage, one reason travelers now prefer night trains is that they want to

questions.prompt: 夜行列車がかつて生き残っていた理由を最もよく表す語句を選んでください。

spans.text: more by nostalgia than by demand

questions.prompt: 第3段落が伝えている主な点はどれですか。
options: Despite high costs and slow tracks, night trains offer comfort and convenience that many value.
Sleeper cars are cheap to run and always arrive ahead of schedule.
Passengers dislike waking up in a different country.
Hotels are now cheaper than any train ticket.

readingQuestions.prompt: 本文を読んで各設問に答えてください。

payload.title: Learning a Language After Forty
paragraphs: A common myth says that adults can never truly master a new language. Children, the story goes, soak up grammar effortlessly, while grown-ups struggle and give up. Recent research tells a more encouraging tale.

Older learners do face real hurdles: busy schedules and a fear of making mistakes in front of others. Yet adults also bring powerful advantages. They understand how grammar works, they can study strategically, and they know exactly why they want to learn. Motivation, it turns out, often matters more than age.

The most successful adult learners share one simple habit. Rather than waiting until they feel ready, they speak from the very first week, accepting that errors are part of progress. Small daily practice, they find, beats rare bursts of intense effort.

questions.prompt: 本文が最も伝えたいことはどれですか。
options: Adults can succeed at language learning, especially with motivation and steady practice.
Only children are capable of learning a new language well.
Studying grammar is useless for adult learners.
Intense weekend study is the best method for everyone.

questions.before: The passage suggests that for adult learners, motivation often matters

questions.sentence: In other words, consistency wins over occasional marathons.

listeningQuestions.transcript: Please remember to back up your files before the system update tonight.
The museum offers free admission on the first Sunday of every month.
Although the proposal sounded promising, the board asked for more detailed figures.
She caught the early train so she could avoid the morning crowds.
The researchers measured a slight but steady rise in ocean temperatures over the decade.
Could you let me know whether the meeting has been moved to Thursday?
